coredata.dc:title: Efficient detection of glucose by graphene-based non-enzymatic sensing material based on carbon dot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jingxin
Wang, Ran
Su, Weiwei
Zhang, Lexin
Li, Adan
Jiao, Tifeng
coredata.dc:description: Diabetes is one of the global chronic diseases, which is mainly caused by the abnormal increase of blood sugar level. Therefore, it is particularly important to monitor glucose in real time. In this work, based on the principle that phenylboronic acid can react with diol rapidly and reversibly in aqueous medium, phenylboronic acid functionalized reduced graphene oxide (rGO-PBA) and polyhydroxy modified carbon spot fluorescent probe (CD1) were designed for detecting important glucose molecules. Among them, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) has high specific surface area and strong fluorescence quenching performance. When CD1 is close to the surface of rGO-PBA, the fluorescence of carbon dots was quenched. With the addition of glucose, the fluorescence of carbon dots increased continuously. The detection of small molecule glucose can be realized quickly and sensitively by using the "on" and "off" strategies of fluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence spectrum
Graphene
Glucose
Carbon dot
Enzyme-free detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of glucose by CDs and rGO-PBA
Morphology characterization of GO and rGO-PBA
Structural characterization of GO and rGO-PBA
Study on the morphology and optical properties of CDs
Determination of glucose by CDs and rGO-PBA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of rGO-PBA and its application in glucose detection.
(a) Photo, (b) TEM and (c) SEM image of GO; (d) Photo, (e) TEM and (f) SEM image of rGO-PBA.
(a) XRD spectra of graphite and GO; (b) Raman spectra of GO and rGO-PBA.
(a) TG curves of GO and rGO-PBA; (b) FT-IR spectra of graphite, GO, 3-PBA and rGO-PBA.

(a) Ultraviolet spectrum of CD1; (b, c) fluorescence emission spectra of CD1 with different preparation time at different excitation wavelengths (320 nm, 360 nm); photos of CD1 under different reaction time (from left to right, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 12 h and 24 h) (d) under sunlight and (e) under ultraviolet lamp.
FT-IR spectra of CD1 and CD2.
XPS spectra of (a) full scan and (b–d)C/N/O elements of CD1.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CD1 quenched by rGO-PBA with different concentrations; (b) detection of glucose by CD1 and rGO-PBA; (c) the dependence curve of glucose concentration; (d) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of fluorescent probes in the presence of glucose and other possible interfering substances.
Comparison of glucose detection in different systems.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a Co3N NW/TM: cobalt nitride nanowire array on Ti mesh; Ag@Fabric: Ag nanoparticle embedded cotton fabric; SERS: Surface enhanced Raman; SPR: Surface Plasmon resonance.

head.ce:title: Efficient detection of glucose by graphene-based non-enzymatic sensing material based on carbon dot

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Metastable Materials Science and Technology, Yanshan University, Qinhuangdao 066004, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Yanshan University, Qinhuangdao 066004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Yanshan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diabetes is one of the global chronic diseases, which is mainly caused by the abnormal increase of blood sugar level. Therefore, it is particularly important to monitor glucose in real time. In this work, based on the principle that phenylboronic acid can react with diol rapidly and reversibly in aqueous medium, phenylboronic acid functionalized reduced graphene oxide (rGO-PBA) and polyhydroxy modified carbon spot fluorescent probe (CD1) were designed for detecting important glucose molecules. Among them, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) has high specific surface area and strong fluorescence quenching performance. When CD1 is close to the surface of rGO-PBA, the fluorescence of carbon dots was quenched. With the addition of glucose, the fluorescence of carbon dots increased continuously. The detection of small molecule glucose can be realized quickly and sensitively by using the "on" and "off" strategies of fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose is the most widely distributed monosaccharide in nature, which is the intermediate product of metabolism in human cells. As the main energy-supplying substance of organisms, the normal level of glucose plays a key role in the normal physiological activities of organisms [1]. As we all know, diabetes is a global chronic disease, and it is one of the major health care problems. The diabetes is mainly caused by the abnormal increase of blood sugar. Therefore, the monitor of blood sugar is very important for the clinical detection and treatment of diabetes.

At present, many effective methods for measuring glucose have been developed to accurately monitor glucose level. For example, optical technology (infrared spectrum, Raman spectrum and fluorescence spectrum) [2,3], surface plasmon resonance technology [4,5], capacitance detection method [6], colorimetric method [7], electrochemical method [8]. Among these technologies, optical technology has attracted many attentions because of its simple operation, low detection limit, good sensitivity and selectivity. Among them, fluorescence technology plays an important role in the field of glucose detection due to its method is simple and fast [9,10]. Generally, the principles of fluorescent probes mainly include photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) of fluorescent molecules in the excited state. Energy transfer (ET) includes electron energy transfer (EET) and FRET. Intramolecular energy can be transferred from the donor chromophore to the acceptor chromophore. Due to the special fluorophore structure of the ICT probe, there is a strong push-pull electron system inside the molecule. Since some atoms or groups constituting the fluorophore can directly participate in the recognition of the guest, when the recognition group is combined with the guest, the intramolecular charge tends to transfer. This will weaken or enhance the fluorescence emission, thus showing a change in fluorescence [11]. In the past, most glucose biosensors were usually prepared based on glucose oxidase or glucose dehydrogenase, which had gradually become attractive platforms due to their high sensitivity and superior selectivity [12,13]. Unfortunately, the performance of glucose sensor based on enzyme is limited to a great extent by enzyme immobilization technology. Meanwhile, external environment such as pH and temperature will greatly affect the biological activity of immobilized enzyme and makes it not stable for a long time.

Natural enzymes are not only expensive, but also limit its application due to their inherent properties. Therefore, researchers have devoted themselves to the research of non-enzymatic glucose biosensors [14]. In the past decades, various nanomaterials with different structures had been synthesized and applied to glucose detection, such as silver nanoparticles, nanoplatform, group IV nanodots (NDs), carbon nanofibers, nanoarray and nano-needles [15–18]. Studies have shown that these nanostructured materials have significantly increased surface area and enhanced physical and chemical activities, but most of their fabrication processes are too cumbersome and difficult to control conditions and functionalize. Comparatively speaking, the preparation methods of CDs are simple and diversified, with easily available raw materials, low cost and adjustable surface function [19,20]. These excellent characteristics provide a larger and broader development space for CDs, which is convenient to the daily life [21,22]. In addition, one of the reasons why carbon dots attract attention is that it has the potential to replace metal-based quantum dots [23]. Metal-based quantum dots have high specific surface area and excellent optical properties, but they generally have inherent biological toxicity. At the same time, the raw materials are expensive and the manufacturing process is difficult to control. However, the essence of CDs is a kind of carbon material with low toxicity and environmental friendliness. Carbon is a basic element in all living organisms with excellent optical properties. Therefore, CDs have been widely attracted more and more attentions [24,25].

Graphene is a new type of two-dimensional carbon nanosheets, which has large specific surface area and strong fluorescence quenching performance. At the same time, graphene shows good stability, which is mainly due to the hybridization of tightly packed carbon atoms and sp2 orbitals [26,27]. In addition, due to the special molecular structure, graphene also has good conductivity and catalytic performance [28,29]. Graphene oxide (GO) is a kind of active graphene sheet obtained by oxidizing a series of active oxygen-containing functional groups [30]. As a derivative of graphene, GO not only has the characteristics of graphene, such as mechanical ductility, electrical conductivity and stability, but also has good biocompatibility and strong hydrophilicity due to the existence of a large number of oxygen-containing groups and carboxyl groups on its surface. Because of its excellent performance, GO plays an important role in biomedicine, electrocatalysis and environmental engineering [31–33].

It is very important to observe the level of glucose in real time due to its important role in biology. This work was designed based on the strong affinity of phenylboronic acid and hydroxyl to enable CD1 to bind phenylboronic acid functionalized reduced graphene oxide (rGO-PBA) to detect glucose. When the fluorescent probe was close to the surface of rGO, the fluorescence would be quenched. At the same time, polyhydroxy carbon dots were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis, which specifically combined with phenylboronic acid. In this study, the strategy of fluorescence "on" and "off" was used to monitor the glucose content more accurately ( Fig. 1).

Graphite powder, 3-aminobenzoic acid, Tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane, citric acid monohydrate, ammonia water, urea, hydrazine monohydrate, glucose, dopamine hydrochloride, ascorbic acid, sodium hydroxide, concentrated sulfuric acid, sodium chloride, potassium nitrate, nitric acid and hydrogen peroxide were all supplied by Aladdin Reagent Factory. Potassium permanganate was from Sigma-Aldrich. Hydrochloric acid and sodium borohydride came from Hebei province Yongfei chemical plant and Alfa Aesar, respectively. Deionized water was obtained from a double-stage Milipore Milli-Q Plus purification system.

GO was prepared by modified Hummers method. First, 1.5 g of potassium nitrate solid and 50 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid were slowly added into a three-necked flask in turn, and then put them in an ice-water bath to fully dissolve under stirring. Next, 1 g graphite powder and 8 g potassium permanganate were added into the above solution in turn, and then stirred to brown paste at a constant temperature of 35 °C. Then, adding 200 mL ultrapure water and keeping at 80 °C for 0.5 h, and naturally cooled to room temperature. 20 mL hydrogen peroxide was added into solution and fully stirred to obtain bright yellow solution. Then, the resulted sample was let stand for stratification and poured out the supernatant. 30 mL hydrochloric acid and proper amount of ultrapure water were added to the solution again and stand for stratification, and this process was repeated many times. Finally, it was washed with ultrapure water and dialyzed to remove unreacted small molecules.

0.21 g of Tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane and 0.21 g of citric acid monohydrate were mixed in 10 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated. Next, placed them in a reaction and kept at 180 °C for 2 h, and then cooled to room temperature to obtain carbon dots 1 (CD1). In addition, 0.5 g urea and 0.16 g citric acid were used to prepare carbon dots 2 (CD2) by repeating the above steps.

250 mg 3-aminophenylboronic acid (3-PBA) was added into 100 mL GO dispersion (0.5 mg/mL) and dissolved under sonication. Then, it was stirred violently at 70 °C for 3 h. Then, adding 0.5 mL of weakly alkaline ammonia water and 0.5 mL of hydrazine monohydrate, and stirring at 80 ℃, continued for 1 h. Under the condition of ammonia water and hydrazine monohydrate, GO can be reduced to rGO and covalently attached to dextran [34]. After the solution was cooled to room temperature, solid NaCl was added and let stand for stratification. Finally, the supernatant was removed and washed with water for several times to obtain rGO-PBA, which was dried for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of glucose by CDs and rGO-PBA
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 0.5 mL different concentrations of rGO-PBA, 0.1 mL CD1 or CD2 and 0.4 mL of ultrapure water were mixed evenly and incubated for 10 min. The fluorescence emission at this time was recorded to determine the concentration of rGO-PBA at the best quenching time. Then, 0.5 mL of rGO-PBA dispersion with optimal concentration, 0.1 mL of CD1 or CD2, and 0.4 mL of glucose solution with different concentrations were mixed evenly, incubated at room temperature for 10 min to record fluorescence spectrum.

The surface morphologies of the samples were observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800Ⅱ) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, HT 7700). The crystal state of the sample was analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, SMART LAB). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS10) was used to analyze the infrared spectra. A confocal Raman microscope (Horiba Jobin Yvon Xplora PLUS) was used to obtain Raman spectra. The UV-Vis spectrum (UV-2600) and fluorescence emission spectrum (F-4500) were used to measure the light absorption and fluorescence emission of samples. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the samples were characterized by BET test (NOVA 4200E). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the elemental composition and valence of the samples. The thermal stability of samples was tested by thermogravimetric analysis (TG, DTG-60) under argon atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology characterization of GO and rGO-PBA
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared GO was brownish yellow powder, as shown in Fig. 2a. A large number of hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups on the surface of GO greatly increased its water solubility. The functional modification of GO with PBA can obtain stable composite. In the reaction of GO with PBA, the nitrogen atom in the amino group donates electrons to GO, resulting in rGO [35]. The micro-morphology of GO and rGO-PBA can be revealed in detail by TEM and SEM. The TEM image in Fig. 2b shows the typical sheet structure of graphene. SEM image shows that GO has a large surface area and many folds, as shown in Fig. 2c, indicating the successful preparation of GO [36]. In addition, the rGO-PBA material was also characterized, as shown in Fig. 2d–f. The rGO-PBA is black powder and show harder morphology than that of GO. It was speculated that the lamellar thickness of the rGO-PBA increases due to the modification of 3-PBA, and the black color of the rGO-PBA also implies that the part of the detached GO was “re-graphitized”. As shown in Fig. 2e, it could be clearly seen that the thickness of rGO-PBA increased compared with GO, and it was composed of gathered wrinkled sheets. Compared with GO, due to the modification of phenylboronic acid on the surface of rGO-PBA, the gap occupation of fold space increased and the interlayer connection was closed, which made the fold decrease (Fig. 2f).

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms can be used to analyze the specific surface area and the pore size distribution of samples [37,38]. As shown in Fig. S1a, at low relative pressure, there was obvious monolayer adsorption, the desorption and adsorption curves basically coincided with each other. At higher relative pressure (P/P0 = 0.8–0.95), the adsorption curve showed a hysteresis loop, which was consistent with H3 hysteresis effect, indicating capillary condensation occurred in adsorbate. The curve showed typical reversible adsorption, which meant the mesoporous of material. When the relative pressure was close to 1, the curve rose, which may be caused by adsorption of adsorbate on some mesoporous. Meanwhile, the pore size distribution curve of rGO-PBA can be obtained by using BJH pore size distribution test method, as shown in Fig. S1b. It could be seen that the pore diameter of the sample was mainly between 2 and 50 nm, which was consistent with BET results. Due to the modification of phenylboronic acid on the surface, the thickness of the sample increased and gaps appeared, which corresponded to its SEM image.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of GO and rGO-PBA
ce:section.ce:para: XRD was an effectively method to analyze and characterize the internal structures of crystals, atoms and molecules [39,40]. Therefore, XRD was performed to further study the peeling state of GO and the synthesis state of rGO-PBA. It could be clearly seen from Fig. 3a that the original graphite had an obvious sharp diffraction peak at 26.52°. By compared with graphite, the characteristic peak at 26.52° disappeared in the spectrum of GO, while a new sharp diffraction peak appeared at 10.60°, proving the successful preparation of GO.

Raman scattering can qualitatively analyze the internal structure and composition information of carbon materials. At the same time, the order degree of crystal or composite materials can be analyzed by the ratio of strength [41,42]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the characteristic diffraction peak corresponds to G band appeared at 1598.83 cm−1, which can be attributed to the first-order scattering of E2 g phonon of sp2 carbon atom. In addition, another characteristic diffraction peak corresponds to D band appeared at about 1371.2 cm−1, and it caused by the separation of lattice vibration from the center of Brillouin zone [43,44]. As for rGO-PBA, the D peak was located at 1359.36 cm−1 and the G peak was located at 1593.07 cm−1, both of which were shifted compared with GO, which might be caused by redox of samples. In addition, there were still unrepaired defects after removing part of oxygen, so the D peak still existed with the decreased intensity.

The element analysis was carried out to further explore the element composition of rGO-PBA. Fig. S2 shows a large number of B elements of rGO-PBA, proving its successful modification. Fig. S2h shows that the color of rGO-PBA and GO solution is black and brown-yellow, respectively. TG was used to further study the thermal stability of the samples under argon atmosphere as shown in Fig. 4a. It can be seen that the mass loss of GO was closed to 100% at 800 °C, while that of rGO-PBA was about 12.2%, indicating that rGO-PBA has higher stability than GO. The mass loss occurred near 100 °C was caused by evaporation of water molecules adsorbed on the material. Then, at about 200 °C, the quality of GO decreased greatly, which was related to the molecular structure of GO. As the temperature increased, the carbonaceous organic components were pyrolyzed. Especially, the existence of unstable oxygen-containing functional groups would cause them to pyrolyze at high temperature, thus lose some small molecules, such as CO, CO2 and a small amount of water molecules. When the temperature reached 240 °C, the loss weight of GO had exceeded 50%, indicating the high surface oxidation degree of GO and the large number of oxygen-containing functional groups. However, the TG curve of rGO-PBA declined gently during the heating process from room temperature to 800 °C with the small loss mass, which was mainly caused by the loss of water, proving the good stability of rGO-PBA. The increased stability after the addition of 3-PBA may be due to the additional boron and carbons from pillared boronic acids increase the bulk between graphene layers [45]. On the other hand, the unstable oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GO were successfully modified by 3-PBA, which enhanced its stability.

FT-IR was used to characterize the internal composition of the sample, as shown in Fig. 4b. There were many characteristic peaks of GO due to the modification of oxygen-containing functional groups. The wide peak in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 was related to the absorption of C-H stretching vibration. The peak derived from the skeleton vibration of CC bond appeared at 1552 cm−1. Compared with GO, a new peak of rGO-PBA appeared at 679 cm−1, which could be due to the out-of-plane bending vibration of N-H of 3-PBA. At the same time, the peak of GO at 1042 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of C-O bond of surface oxygen-containing group. Compared with GO, 3-PBA and rGO-PBA had a characteristic peak at 767 cm−1, which might be caused by O-H out-of-plane bending on benzene sulfonic acid group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the morphology and optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Polyhydroxy carbon dots (CD1) were prepared by hydrothermal method by mixing citric acid monohydrate with tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane in a molar ratio of 1:1. As shown in Fig. 5, it was observed that typical nanoparticles were uniformly dispersed with smooth edge and regular circle. The existence of two kinds of particles with different sizes was speculated to be due to the heat transfer from outside to inside, uneven heating, and the existence of polyhydroxy groups, resulted in intermolecular force and easy agglomeration.

In order to explore the optical properties of CD1, a series of spectral characterizations were carried out. When the light-absorbing solution was irradiated with ultraviolet light or visible monochromatic light, it would absorb the incident light to different degrees, which could be qualitatively analyzed according to the change of absorbance with wavelength. From Fig. 6a, it could be seen that the maximum absorption appeared at about 320 nm, indicating that CD1 got more light energy here. The steady-state fluorescence spectrum was the superposition of photons emitted from a system received multiple times within a certain period of time, and light absorption was usually accompanied by light emission. Therefore, excitation wavelengths of 320 nm and 360 nm were selected for excitation, as shown in Figs. 6b and 6c. It could be found that the fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm was higher than that at 360 nm, which may be due to the strong ultraviolet absorption leading to the emission of high-intensity light. In addition, with the change of reaction time, the fluorescence emission intensity also changed, and the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum when the reaction time was 2 h. Once the reaction time was too long, the color of the product became dark, and the fluorescence intensity decreased. It was speculated that the substance might be carbonized and agglomerated, thus caused fluorescence quenching. However, too short reaction time would result in incomplete reaction, which showed weak fluorescence emission. These phenomena could be also reflected in Figs. 6d and 6e.

In addition, CD2 was prepared by the same method using citric acid and urea as a comparative experiment, and its morphology was observed by TEM. It could be seen from Figs. S3a and 3b that the carbon dots were typical nanoparticles, which were uniformly dispersed in the solution with a size of about 4 nm. Fig. S3d showed the ultraviolet spectra of CD2 with a wide absorption at around 300–500 nm. Different fluorescence excitation wavelengths were set corresponding to ultraviolet absorption, as shown in Fig. S3e. A typical excitation wavelength dependence was found, which could be well applied to optical devices. In addition, the fluorescence emission spectra of CD2 with reaction time was also explored. It could be seen from Fig. S4a that the fluorescence intensity was basically the same at 12 h and 24 h, and in order to keep the uniformity with larger size, the samples reacted for 24 h were took for subsequent tests. The change of fluorescence intensity could also be observed by Fig. S3c, in which the upper picture was took under sunlight and the lower picture was took under ultraviolet lamp. Under the irradiation of ultraviolet lamp, the fluorescence intensity increased with the increase in reaction time. Under sunlight, the color of sample solution became lighter with the reaction time increased, and it was speculated that the decrease of fluorescence intensity was related to the aggregation of carbon nanoparticles [46].

FT-IR tests were carried out to further study the structure of CDs, as shown in Fig. 7. The infrared peaks at 3192 cm−1 and 3040 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C-H, and the peak at 2830 cm−1 was related to the stretching absorption of carboxylic acid. In the FT-IR spectra of CD1, the peaks at 1539 cm−1 and 1372 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration absorption of CO bond and the bending vibration of CH bond, respectively. In addition, the infrared characteristic peak at 1046 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of CO bond, and the O-H corresponded to alcohol at 613 cm−1 bends out of plane, which indicated that alcohol hydroxyl groups were introduced. In the spectrum of CD2, a strong peak appeared at 1652 cm−1, which was attributed to the N-H bending vibration of amide, indicating the introduction of amino group. In addition, the peaks at 1394 cm−1 and 1182 cm−1 were caused by the bending vibration of C-H and the stretching vibration of C-N, respectively.

XPS can usually show the chemical interface bonding state of various composite materials [47,48]. Fig. 8 showed the XPS spectra of CD1, three peaks could be seen at 284.47 eV, 400.41 eV and 531.88 eV, corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8a, there were four peaks of 283.64 eV, 284.8 eV, 286.35 eV and 288.74 eV in C1s spectrum, which might be due to sp2 hybridized C atom (CC bond, CO bond) and CO bond in lattice respectively. As shown in Fig. 8c, there were two different types of nitrogen doping in N1s spectrum, among which 397.92 eV and 399.64 eV belonged to pyrrole nitrogen and 401.5 eV belonged to graphite nitrogen. As for O element, the peaks at 531.04 eV and 531.32 eV were related to CO bond, while the peaks at 531.86 eV and 532.61 eV corresponded to COC and COH bond, respectively (Fig. 8d). Therefore, there were hydroxyl groups on the surface of CD1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of glucose by CDs and rGO-PBA
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that phenylboronic acid had strong affinity with hydroxyl groups, therefore, this work used CD1 combined with rGO-PBA to detect glucose, as shown in Fig. 9. When carbon nanoparticles were closed to the surface of rGO-PBA, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched, as shown in Fig. 9a. The fluorescence signals of various fluorophores can be effectively quenched by GO and graphene. The fluorescence quenching of GO is based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or photoinduced electron transfer (PET). When GO and fluorophore are close to each other, energy or excited electrons will be transferred from the fluorophore to the GO, thereby quenching the fluorescence signal. In the presence of the appropriate analyte, the fluorophore is separated from the GO surface by a stronger interaction, and the fluorescence signal is recovered. After adding the appropriate analyte, the fluorophore is separated from the GO surface through stronger interactions, and the fluorescence signal is recovered [49]. In this work, rGO-PBA forms boronate esters with CD1. The fluorescence is quenched when the fluorescent molecules close to rGO-PBA. After adding glucose molecules, it can form strong boronate esters with rGO-PBA than that of the CD1. Therefore, the CD1 will go far away from the rGO-PBA surface, and the fluorescence will come back or fluorescence is on. In addition, the fluorescence signal depends on the amount of glucose added to the fluorescence-quenched state [50]. It could be clearly seen that the fluorescence intensity increased with the increase of glucose molecules, as shown in Fig. 9b. At the same time, in order to more intuitively observe the detection effect of rGO-PBA on glucose, the glucose concentration-dependent curve was explored, as shown in Fig. 9c. In which F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of CD1 and rGO-PBA before and after adding glucose solution. It could be seen that the solution before and after adding glucose had a good linear relationship between the glucose concentration and (F-F0)/F0 with glucose in the range of 2.5–70 mg/mL. The previously reported works about glucose detection are shown in Table 1 [51–56]. It is worth noting that the preparation of the enzyme-based glucose sensor has a high cost, the processing of the enzyme is complicated. In addition, glucose electrochemical sensors show a relatively low catalytic activity. The rGO-PBA preparation method used in this work is simple and low-cost.

In addition, the same experimental explorations were also carried out on CD2, as shown in Fig. S4. When CD2 solution was mixed with rGO-PBA dispersed liquid phase, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of fluorescent molecules also occurred due to the characteristic of GO. However, when glucose was added again, the fluorescence intensity was obviously enhanced, but there was no good linear relationship between glucose concentration and (F-F0)/F0 before and after adding glucose. The results indicated that the fluorescence sensitivity of CD2 was not well applied to glucose detection, while CD1 was feasible for glucose detection. At the same time, the interference experiments of glucose detection were carried out by small molecular substances such as ascorbic acid, l-cysteine and dopamine. Fig. 9d showed that when glucose molecules existed, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was higher than that of interfering substances. Therefore, the research showed that rGO-PBA based on CD1 could be used for the detection of glucose molecules.

In summary, rGO-PBA and polyhydroxy-modified nano-fluorescent carbon dots were successfully prepared in this work to detect glucose molecules. 3-PBA was prepared by hydrazine monohydrate and ammonia water, and Polycarbonyl carbon dots (CD1) were prepared by simple hydrothermal synthesis. The characteristics of graphene can be remained after a series of redox reactions, which provides a guarantee for the detection of glucose. The prepared CD1 can interact with rGO-PBA to realize the accurate detection of glucose using the "on" and "off" strategy of nano-sensor fluorescence. Therefore, the current research work provides a reference for future glucose detection.

Jingxin Zhou: Performed the experiments and write paper. Ran Wang: Data curation. Weiwei Su: Data curation. Lexin Zhang: Data curation. Adan Li: Data curation. Tifeng Jiao: Designed the experiments, review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21872119, 22072127), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Nos. B2021203016, B2021203005), the Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department (No. ZD2022147), and the Special Project for Local Science and Technology Development Guided by the Central Government of China (No. 216Z1301G).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129122.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles as peroxidase mimetics and their applications in H2O2 and glucose detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wei, E. Wang, Fe3O4 Magnetic Nanoparticles as Peroxidase Mimetics and Their Applications in H2O2 and Glucose Detection, Anal. Chem. 80 (2008) 2250–2254.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets: a novel peroxidase mimetic, Fe doping-mediated catalytic performance enhancement and application to rapid, highly sensitive optical detection of glucose
